LAW AND MOTION 302, DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT IS GRANTED. THE COURT DID NOT CONSIDER THE NEW EVIDENCE SUBMITTED IN REPLY. PLAINTIFF DID NOT ESTABLISH A PECULIAR DANGEROUS CONDITION THAT WOULD REQUIRE DEFENDANT TO LIGHT THE INTERSECTION. (MIXON V. PG&E (2012) 207 CAL.APP.4TH 124, 133.) DESIGN IMMUNITY SHIELDS THE CITY FROM ANY LIABILITY ARISING FROM THE PLACEMENT OF THE LIMIT LINE OR PEDESTRIAN SIGNAL POLE. (GOVT. CODE. SEC. 830.6.) SIGN IMMUNITY SHIELDS THE CITY FROM ANY LIABILITY ARISING FROM LACK OF SIGNAGE AT THE INTERSECTION. (GOVT. CODE SECS. 830.4 AND 830.8.) PLAINTIFF HAS NOT ESTABLISHED A TRIABLE ISSUE OF MATERIAL FACT AS TO WHETHER THE FADED CROSSWALK LINE AMOUNTS TO A DANGEROUS CONDITION, AS DEFINED IN GOVT. CODE SEC. 830. ALTHOUGH PLAINTIFF'S EXPERT SPECULATES THAT THE DRIVER OF THE VEHICLE MAY HAVE BEEN UNSURE WHERE TO STOP, THE DEPOSITION TESTIMONY OF THE DRIVER ESTABLISHES THAT THE DRIVER WAS FAMILIAR WITH THE INTERSECTION AND KNEW WHERE THE CROSSWALK WAS LOCATED. (DECLARATION OF DAVID A. DELBON, EX. C, DEPOSITION OF NAHIL SHERZOY, 37:1-38:18, 40:10-17, 52:7-18.) FURTHER, PLAINTIFF CANNOT DEMONSTRATE A SUBSTANTIAL RISK OF INJURY WHEN DUE CARE IS USED BECAUSE A DRIVER USING DUE CARE SHOULD YIELD TO PEDESTRIANS IN AN INTERSECTION EVEN WITHOUT A MARKED CROSSWALK PURSUANT TO VEH. CODE SEC. 21950. ORDER SIGNED IN OPEN COURT.
PROOF OF SERVICE OF MOTION FILED BY PLAINTIFF LOGAN, BRYAN

LAW AND MOTION 302, NO APPEARANCE ON THIS MATTER. THE COURT ADOPTED ITS TENTATIVE RULING. PLAINTIFF BRYAN LOGAN'S MOTION FOR RECONSIDERATION OF MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT IS CONTINUED TO MAY 16, 2014. DEPARTMENT 302 IS NO LONGER IN RECEIPT OF THE COURTESY COPIES OF THE PAPERS FILED IN SUPPORT AND OPPOSITION OF THE UNDERLYING MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT. DEPARTMENT 302 DOES NOT HAVE ACCESS TO THE COURT FILE TO VIEW THESE DOCUMENTS. PLAINTIFF, THE PARTY MOVING FOR RECONSIDERATION, MUST PROVIDE TO DEPARTMENT 302 A COURTESY COPY OF ALL PAPERS FILED IN SUPPORT AND OPPOSITION OF THE UNDERLYING MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT WITH A COVER LETTER REFLECTING THE NEW HEARING DATE BY APRIL 21, 2014 AT 4:00 P.M. OR THE MOTION MAY BE DENIED. ANY EXHIBIT MUST BE SEPARATED BY AN EXHIBIT TAB PER CRC 3.1110(F). JUDGE: ERNEST H. GOLDSMITH, CLERK: CYNTHIA HERBERT, NOT REPORTED.

calendar.matters:
CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Sep-08-2014 Off Calendar

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Jul-08-2014 Continued To Sep-24-14 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

Plaintiff'S Motion For Reconsideration
Off Calendar Jun-12-2014 Off Calendar
MINI MINUTES: Plaintiff'S Motion For Reconsideration is off calendar. Duplicate entry.

Mtn For Reconsideration Of Mtn For Summ Judgmnt; Notice Of Lodgment
Off Calendar Jun-12-2014 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES: No appearance on this matter. RULING: The Court adopted its tentative ruling.

RULING: The Court adopted its tentative ruling. PLAINTIFF BRYAN LOGAN's motion For Reconsideration Of Motion For Summary Judgment is granted. Pursuant to Le Francois v. Goel (2005) 35 Cal.4th 1094, the Court vacates the February 21, 2014 Order and corrects it as follows:
Defendant's motion for summary judgment is granted. The Court did not consider the new evidence submitted in reply. Plaintiff did not establish a peculiar dangerous condition that would require Defendant to light the intersection. (Mixon v. PG&E ((2012) 207 Cal.App.4th 124, 133.) Design immunity shields the City from any liability arising from the placement of the limit line or pedestrian signal pole. (Govt. Code. sec. 830.6.) Sign immunity shields the City from any liability arising from lack of signage at the intersection. (Govt. Code secs. 830.4 and 830.8.) Plaintiff has not established a triable issue of material fact as to whether the faded crosswalk line amounts to a dangerous condition, as defined in Govt. Code sec. 830(a). The Court finds that, viewing the evidence most favorably to the plaintiff, the risk created by the faded crosswalk line was of such a minor, trivial, or insignificant nature in view of the surrounding circumstances that no reasonable person could conclude that the condition created a substantial risk of injury when such property or adjacent property was used with due care in a manner in which it was reasonably foreseeable that it would be used. (Gov. Code sec. 830.2.) It is undisputed that the intersection is controlled by traffic lights and pedestrian signals which govern the right of way of all users. (UMF no. 12). In the approximate ten years before the subject accident, there were no reported pedestrian collisions in the crosswalk where Plaintiff was struck. (UMF no. 21.) Plaintiff sets forth no evidence refuting this fact. (See Fielder v. City of Glendale (1977) 71 Cal.App.3d 719, 734 in determining whether a condition is dangerous "the court should see if there is any evidence that other persons have been injured on this same defect.") Further, the pictures of the intersection show that the crosswalk line is faded in parts, but is not "non-existent," as asserted by Plaintiff's expert. (Declaration of William R. Neuman, 6(A)(1), Exs. 3-4; see also Declaration of Brian Woo, Ex. D.) The parts of the first line of the crosswalk that are not faded clearly delineate the existence of the first crosswalk line, which is in the driver's line of sight prior to and during the turn. (Id.)The Court finds that the partially faded crosswalk line does not create a substantial risk of injury if an individual drives with due care at the signal-controlled intersection. Alternatively, even if Plaintiff could establish that the faded crosswalk line amounted to a dangerous condition, the dangerous condition must be the proximate cause of the injury for liability to arise. (Gov. Code sec. 835.) Veh. Code 21950 states that a driver of a vehicle "shall yield the right-of-way to a pedestrian crossing the roadway within any marked crosswalk or within any unmarked crosswalk at an intersection'" Thus, as argued by Defendant, regardless of whether there was a marked crosswalk, the driver still had to yield to crossing pedestrians. The pedestrian crossing signals are lined up with the first crosswalk line; thus the driver should have known she had an obligation to stop for pedestrians. (Woo Decl., Ex. D.) The evidence establishes that the driver's failure to comply with the Vehicle Code proximately caused Plaintiff's injuries. Order signed in open court. Judge: Ernest H. Goldsmith, Clerk: Lesley Fiscella, Reporter: Sherry Sawyer #5976.

MASTER CALENDAR JURY
Off Calendar Mar-06-2014 Set For Case Management Conference On Jun-04-14 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
Off Calendar Feb-21-2014 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances: Mary Alexander, Esq. and Jennifer Fiore, Esq., 415.433.4440, for plaintiff Bryan Logan.
David Delbon, Deputy City Attorney, for defendant City and County of San Francisco.

RULING: Argued and the Court adopted its tentative ruling: DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT is granted. The Court did not consider the new evidence submitted in reply. Plaintiff did not establish a peculiar dangerous condition that would require Defendant to light the intersection. (Mixon v. PG&E (2012) 207 Cal.App.4th 124, 133.) Design immunity shields the City from any liability arising from the placement of the limit line or pedestrian signal pole. (Govt. Code. sec. 830.6.) Sign immunity shields the City from any liability arising from lack of signage at the intersection. (Govt. Code secs. 830.4 and 830.8.) Plaintiff has not established a triable issue of material fact as to whether the faded crosswalk line amounts to a dangerous condition, as defined in Govt. Code sec. 830. Although Plaintiff's expert speculates that the driver of the vehicle may have been unsure where to stop, the deposition testimony of the driver establishes that the driver was familiar with the intersection and knew where the crosswalk was located. (Declaration of David A. Delbon, Ex. C, Deposition of Nahil Sherzoy, 37:1-38:18, 40:10-17, 52:7-18.) Further, Plaintiff cannot demonstrate a substantial risk of injury when due care is used because a driver using due care should yield to pedestrians in an intersection even without a marked crosswalk pursuant to Veh. Code sec. 21950. Order signed in open court. Judge: Ernest Goldsmith, Clerk: Lesley Fiscella, Reporter: Sherry Sawyer, CSR5976
